speaker: interviewee
text: When the bunker-- it-- and then in the summer, we had to stock up from summer the food because in the winter was harder to get food yet because if you walked on the snow it would show prints, and then they could follow in there. So all summer long, whatever we could do, we'd stock up for the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been cold in the winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was not too bad and the bunker, no. It's very deep in the ground. We'd have something, like, to cook or whatever, but you couldn't get out too much-- what do you call it-- smoke from the stove. You know what I think they do is most of the time they did it there in the day, and at night it would show up more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get organized to be fighting groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us about half a year because must of them were on a train. And the Russians, when they come over, they had the only guns. And since that's where they're going to make a they thought they're going to be getting a couple Germans, we'd get a couple more guns. But later on, we did find some of them. And some of them are other-- other groups were fighting with them, and they got rid of a couple of them. And they left their guns in the woods, and we kept picked them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your leader? Did you have a leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exact. No. It was-- everybody was, like, together. You know what I mean? I realize-- I don't remember. I don't think there was strictly a guy that we were asking most of the time for anything they want to do as volunteers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you volunteer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. Not all the time. Everybody did go, you know, because there was a very dangerous mission. You volunteer. But then the next time you said, well, I've been here twice. Why don't you get him once? And I mean, everybody, even if you don't want it, you had to do it though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any close calls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's the close call I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got shot? Were you able to get any news about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. You were cut off from the world. You only-- a lot of-- some of them could escape into town and check up on things what goes on. And not-- the Gentile could do it though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would they go? To Miedzyrzecz or to [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, to Miedzyrzecz because they are Gentile. You know, they recognized who was-- who was Jewish and who not.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the forest, did you all get along? The Jews and the Gentiles and the Russians and the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles, no. There were no Poles altogether. And one time, we had, like, two of them. And they did go out for some mission, and it didn't come through. So they think maybe they turned them in. Well, they said to them, they don't want them. You have 48 hours. Find yourself another group. I mean, they didn't punish them or anything. They were-- didn't enough proof. But they tell them, there's other groups over here. Probably there was more than one, you know. So they tell them to hook up with another group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a gun of your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a plain gun, a single bullet. Like you're here. Over here you've got-- what do you call it? Hunting-- hunting-- what do you call it? A single bullet gun, you pull the- the trigger in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you kill any Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We-- we had-- we thought we did, but I didn't go back to check out. I think we on one mission was successful. We could pick up a couple of guns with that. And later, I hear that it-- that it was so many. But it was hard to prove it [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the hardest thing about being in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hardest thing is the sickness and food because I remember over there towards the end of the war, I got dysentery, or something like that. And before I know, the next guy got it and the next guy. And there was no medicine out to treat it. That was the hardest thing though. And a lot of them died because of that. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of your good friends among them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you would bury them in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think, I mean, it was a couple-- a couple of Jewish people, a boy and a man. And I talked to them, and then I turned around, and they are gone. A couple of them, at least four or five. And you just didn't never-- I don't know. It turned bad. No-- and they didn't have no medicine out to control it. But somehow, I don't know, I made it. Don't ask me how or what. I probably-- maybe-- maybe I didn't eat much. And they were hungry, and they-- I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's like everything. It's like they say in Jewish, it's [NON-ENGLISH]. You don't know. I was not any better than the rest of them. I know that much. But that's the way it's supposed to be. What can I tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut off from the world the way you were, what did you think was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I tell you, you're in there, and then some of them-- some of them, they were not your-- OK Russian and did go out. And they said they hear that the Russians are moving. And they say there's so many kilometers. And we got to hold out till they came. Then things will be at least a little better. You know, like now food is the main-- the main thing. And then they come and say, well, they are 200 miles, or whatever, 200 kilometers. In the next three days, they should be in our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Being so hungry, did you talk about food a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's all you talk about. You get everybody-- you have, like, a bread, and you slice it up. And everybody gets-- and get that piece of bread. And then the other guy turns around, and he calls the name. So everybody get the equal parts in that. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you take a bread and cut it up in 14 pieces. And-- and everybody at the table looks, and he says, well, which piece looks bigger or smaller? And he says, well, this one is a little bigger. This is smaller. And then you turn around-- and you turn around there, and you call each name. So and everybody says, is it equal like that? Yeah? OK, now we're going to call.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody turns around. He says, well, this man, this man, everybody get it. And then they have a little soup and that and-- that will do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the food for the whole day? Soup and bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soup and bread, that's about it sometimes. One day was better than the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any meat or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometime they could catch some animal there in the woods. But there was-- they were afraid to shoot there. You know what I mean? When you're shoot a hollow thing, you can hear it too. If you were going in the woods and shoot, you can hear a big fire there. And when you are hiding, you don't want the enemy to know where you're hiding. A couple of times, the Germans tried to get in with the dogs. And they went so far, and they couldn't see nothing. And they turned right back. But two, three times they tried, with a Ukrainian and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they thought this time, OK, there-- there it is. And he said, the guy-- you know they always have one guy watching what goes on, like, two, three people. Are they moving? And they are certain signals-- hand signals or whatever-- you tell it to the other one. And anyway, this is the-- this is the time I thought that they were very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, they were afraid to go in because most of the time when they got in too deep, they didn't get out anyway. If-- you know, they just got-- they-- they killed them. So I didn't want to go in too deep.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point in the forest, did you stop being afraid all of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At what? What?

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point in the forest-- in the beginning you were afraid all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then at some point, it stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: At some point-- at some point where you know you-- you lost everybody and that your life isn't worth nothing anyway. You know what I'm trying to say? You don't have much. You only have the-- the couple of people that you know about. Beside that, you just-- you just don't have nobody. It was hard even in, like, in when the Germans come in and you-- you walk on the sidewalk, and he said to me, you there! We want you to walk on the-- on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the ghetto time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They don't let you walk on the-- on the sidewalk any--

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the ghetto. I didn't ask you. Did you have a job in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here and there, yeah. I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, nothing, just manual jobs. I remember one time in their winter, they got stuck, and then they couldn't bring nothing in or out. You had to shovel out all this. They didn't have the modern equipment. I had to go and shovel snow. I worked six hours there. It was not only me, but others. It was blowing around like your house so deep. They had to take and shovel out the snow to make-- to bring in supplies or whatever. But they supposed to pay you though. But then they-- they paid some but very little though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the forest, you were there all alone. But did you want to live? You said it was [INAUDIBLE] that you did live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But then, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you want to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when you have-- when you hook up with a group and one-- and once you ever-- they-- how do you say? Gave a lot of fire where you'd be wanting [INAUDIBLE]. And he needs-- tells to the other one, listen, we made it so far. We're going to make it regardless of what. What-- what if you were dead? They didn't have a chance altogether. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you would make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [CRYING] No, I didn't think so. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the worst moment for you in the whole geshefter.

speaker: interviewee
text: In what? Like?

speaker: interviewer
text: The worst. The very worst moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hardest thing was being separated from the parents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's number one. But then you had to fight for survival was number two. And then when I get sick was number three. I put this on there last. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, was-- was-- and as soon the ration come in, sort of, and they give me-- what do you call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Medical care.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they come in? When was the world for--

speaker: interviewee
text: In '43 they came in. '43? '40-- '43? Know '44? It's not so clear in my mind. I think it's '40-- '44, in the beginning of '44 they were. Yeah, '44. In the beginning of-- in February of '44, they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went over to the town. And then, we could hear-- we could hear a lot of rumbling-- you know the tanks and that. And so OK, we send out a group of people. And find out what happened. And he says, forget it. It's all over. You cannot go at least wherever you want to go. But I didn't stop during the time altogether. The time when I was born, I didn't because I-- I find out there's nothing in there. There's not-- nobody alive, so we're not going to stop there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians came, were you able to walk again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I needed medical attention. And then, right away they give me that medical attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they put you in a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Field hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There for quite a bit. I couldn't-- they-- I had-- we did-- they didn't have nothing neither. They told the lungs filled up with-- they had to poke me with some needles and get-- jars like that. What do you call it, pleurisy or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had to take the fluid out.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fluid out. And there was no medicine to dry it up so I had to stay there for six weeks. And then after it was all done, you just-- you're too weak though. But I will keep moving with them because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now, we did find out that in, like, in Poland, these groups of Jewish Haganah, I guess. And they going to help us to resettle or whatever. But they are afraid to operate in Russia because the Russians don't let-- they call them the Zionists. And they would catch them. They just going to put them away to Siberia. He says, whatever you guys want to do. You want to stay here, you stay here. But if you want to go, we're going to find you a place-- not in Poland whatever, but in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what happened. They worked underground. When you come in Poland there, I mean, they were helping out. You were on easy street already. And you were getting all the medical and everything because it's come a lot of-- from Israel or around the country would come all kind of-- like, but me if they come there, I had all the medicine that I needed. I got well in no time. Where did you go? Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Szczecin then. By that time they-- they kicked out the Germans. I think that was [NON-ENGLISH]. Szczecin, yeah. It used to be the Germans, and then they come in. And then they kicked them out. And they gave it-- and it still was-- years ago, it was Poland, OK? Then the Germans took it away. And then they-- after the-- the war was over, they kicked them back out, and Germany took them all in.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now, I'm a little bit confused. You were going with the Russian army--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but after a certain point, then the Russian and the Polish were the same. But you couldn't operate. Like, if you were a Zionist or whatever under Russian territory, you couldn't operate and say you-- you were a Zionist. A Zionist to them is like-- like you're a spy for them-- for them or something. So they told us after--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Szczecin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with the Army still, or you were on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's it. I was feeling good. I didn't need them then. We were staying in there. But then they organized groups of people, youth, and they wanted to bring to Israel. I think it was about six of us. We were six, and we were together. And every day, we stayed together. There was no-- no women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they-- the six?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are all in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember all of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I'm thinking it, but it don't come through that clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the six of you were together in Szczecin. And what were you planning to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you planning to do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they set us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Haganah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Haganah. They said, you're going to stay here till we have another place we're going to move you. Just stay here. We're going to feed you and everything. And you don't have to do nothing, which they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, from Szczecin, we went to I think Czechoslovakia. And there, we stayed. Well, there-- there was better. Every time we made a move, was that life turned better a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czechoslovakia, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia, we-- we stayed in a camp-- I forgot, I can't remember-- where the Jewish people got, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a DP camp or a kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a-- a kibbutz, not a DP camp. It was a concentration camp, and they let us stay there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they got to where they-- sure it wasn't camp? I can't say. But anyway, in Czechoslovakia, they helped us out. We stayed-- we stayed until they were ready for us in-- in Germany. And then they transported us back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told you. Bad Reichenhall. From Czechoslovakia to Bad Reichenhall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what went on in Bad Reichenhall? Describe that for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm first in the kibbutz. Though I don't figure the kibbutz because we didn't stay there too long. I feel I was at Bad Reichenhall because most of the time, from 1946 to '49, I spent there most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in Bad Reichenhall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, life was not too bad. You were getting from UNRRA, you were getting a package every-- you know what I mean? You're getting a package. And a lot of packages come from the United States for the sick people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in dormitories there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This was a-- a military camp. And it's a German military camp. And then they kicked them out, and they turned it into a DP camp. And every family had one room only. I think we had one room. And we split it up in half. It was me and my brother. And the other was-- was five in one room. And you have a-- in the basement you have a kitchen. And-- and everybody eats there. I mean, you're single-- all the single people eat in the mess. And probably there were some people helping us out to leave the food in there. Some used to make some for Shabbos, you know. Although, most people didn't have nothing so that buyed whatever it is, and then they make it. I remember they made all kind of stuff for us for the Shabbos. But over there, I was less religious than I was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Things changed very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was in the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to get in the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: They needed somebody. Well, I mean I-- when you think maybe the police, like in the ghetto, we didn't-- I didn't-- you didn't have no power. People are well, all you have to do is have order, you know. If you say a man and a wife, they have a fight, or you or someone have to stop them. And you have to watch, like, contraband not to get in there, whatever. There's some people they like to, you know, they bring in, like, cigarettes or something. They bring them in anyway. So that you stay by the door. And they cut a hole by the fence, and they get through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever arrest anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too lenient, and I got bawled out a lot of times. So they put me in a place where nobody was around there, you know. At the back door, there was nobody there. It's quiet. You know, they checked up in there. I suppose there's some-- some woman was crying in there. I looked up. She shouldn't have it-- whatever. And I say, OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So someone turned me in though. So he said, instead of firing you from the job, they are going to give you-- they are going to put you always in the back over there. So this way, you wouldn't have no problem. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When, when you were getting extra money for that? By doing that? Yeah, I would. I got fired. I told them to chuck it. Well, I think I'd gotten $25 a week or something. And maybe-- that's about it, I guess. Yeah. $25 or $30? I don't remember. Now, a lot of people, when they got in the camps, got married right away. Hello, how are you? And the huppah. But you didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not till-- what?

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you didn't get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was kind of young. I didn't I find nobody. There was not too many women. Do you ever realize that? Anyone that-- where the girls there was available, the older men picked them. [LAUGHING] Yeah, I remember that. When there was young women and the older men grabbed them the younger women. I didn't know much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was-- when I was in Germany, I think I was 20. Eh? Well, the war was over '45, '46. You were 23.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK. And I remember most of the women were maybe 23 or 25. And the men were like 30. And they-- they probably know what was going on than me at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a girlfriend ever, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-uh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened after Bad Reichenhall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad Reichenhall, I come back here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come back to Appleton, Wisconsin. They sponsored me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sponsored you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish-- what do you call it? The Jewish-- UNRRA or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Joint or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Joint Distribution Committee. Who did you come to here? I told you. Didn't I tell you how I find a relative here? I come to Appleton, and I stayed about three months. Or two months. The-- the-- Joint Distribution Committee says go over there. And they-- they'll find a job for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shovel coal. [LAUGHS] In a-- in a stock room there. But I didn't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know that they-- they asked me, why were you-- you know, you come out of the job. You're all black. And they all asked me, you like it? I say, yeah. How's the job? The job-- you know, as a young man I used to work hard. Is the job hard? No. Then the men-- the men from the store said, you know what? You're going to know a little more English. I have a better job for you. This is a starting job. I want to see if-- how good you're going to do with that. And then he says we're going to move you up. You can be a salesman or something. And I says good. And the main thing there was, I'm not a Jewish guy. He's a refugee. He's younger than me. So he said to me, you know what? We're going to go to Milwaukee. Do you want to, I mean, do you want to go with us? He had a car. I didn't have a car. I didn't have a car till 1955. I figured I don't have no money. I don't need a car. And he had a sweet car. But I mean, I bought a car when I had cash stored. And I worked hard, and I had enough money. But I bought a car. To make the story short, he took me to Milwaukee. And then, when you are a refugee, you'll come-- there's four people who says, what city are you? And then one asked the other one, what city are you? And you say you are from a town like that. Do you know anybody from my town? I mean, how are you going-- there's no communication. There is no telephone. So you would-- by the people, between the people, you communicate and find out what town they are, or who is alive now of what and everything. That's why-- or you put your name in wherever you go. You put your name, and people, you know, at certain places like in the kibbutz, or some other kibbutz. And they check. And then you come, the first thing you check to see-- who is alive. And that's-- what did I say now? I forgot.
